nativeRange.habitat: Upper reef slopes, mid-slopes, and lagoon environments; also found in non-reefal areas with muddy or mixed substrates — less restricted to high-energy reef crests than Acropora

decorPreferences: open rockwork placement with spacing from neighbors

diet.notes: Primarily photosynthetic via zooxanthellae. Supplemental feeding is optional — some keepers offer very small meaty foods such as rotifers, or dose amino acids. Many successful reef keepers report good growth without targeted feeding. As colonies grow substantially, consumption of calcium, alkalinity, and trace elements increases significantly and dosing regimes should be adjusted accordingly.

avoidWith: corals placed in direct contact (colony shading and overcrowding)
aggressive LPS sweeper tentacle corals without spacing

summary: Anacropora is a niche SPS genus in family Acroporidae, distinguished from Acropora by the absence of axial corallites, producing thin, fusing branches. Generally considered more forgiving than Acropora; advanced-level care.
careNotes:
Note: Anacropora is a genus of approximately 7 described species sold at the genus level in the aquarium trade; all share near-identical husbandry requirements. The most commonly traded specimen is ORA-aquacultured Neon Green Anacropora.

Morphological distinction from Acropora: The definitive feature separating Anacropora from the visually similar Acropora is the absence of axial corallites (the dominant terminal polyp at the tip of each branch that characterizes Acropora). Anacropora branches terminate without this enlarged apical polyp, giving branch tips an 'empty' or Montipora-like appearance. Radial corallites are small and rounded (up to ~1 mm diameter). As colonies mature, branches fuse together into a dense 'briar patch' structure — hence the common trade name 'Briar Coral.'

Key care requirements:
• Lighting: Generally advised at 150–350 PAR, which overlaps with Acropora but can tolerate slightly lower minimums. Higher light tends to produce lighter colony coloration; medium light (100–200 PAR) tends toward richer browns with visible polyp color. Acclimate new frags gradually from lower positions upward over 2–4 weeks.
• Flow: Moderate to high non-laminar flow is required. Flow becomes increasingly important as colonies densify — interior polyps of a mature briar-patch colony receive less water movement, so powerhead placement and gyre-type flow are recommended to maintain circulation throughout the colony.
• Water chemistry stability: Anacropora shares Acropora's sensitivity to alkalinity swings and calcium/magnesium fluctuations. A mature system (6+ months) is generally advised before introducing SPS like Anacropora. Typical successful parameters: temperature 24–27°C, salinity SG 1.024–1.026, pH 8.1–8.4, alkalinity 8–10 dKH, calcium 380–450 ppm, magnesium 1250–1450 ppm.
• Care level: Two Bulk Reef Supply listings rate Anacropora 'moderate,' and TankFacts describes it as 'fairly hardy compared to some SPS.' Anacropora is generally considered more forgiving than Acropora — it adapts to a wider PAR range and tolerates slightly less pristine water. Despite this, it is classified here as 'advanced' because it is still an SPS coral requiring stable alkalinity, calcium, and magnesium dosing, an established reef system, and regular water-quality monitoring. Beginner reef keepers attempting Anacropora without established SPS husbandry practices tend to have poor outcomes.
• Spacing and growth: Anacropora grows rapidly and colonies can expand significantly, shading and overcrowding neighboring corals. Plan for long-term growth — provide ample space on the rockwork from the outset.
• Aggression: Low direct aggression but can shade neighbors as colonies expand. Maintain spacing of at least 3–5 cm from other corals.
• Pest prevention: Dip new frags (e.g., CoralRx, Revive) as a precaution against Acropora-eating flatworms (AEFW), which have been documented affecting Anacropora due to its close family relationship with Acropora.
• Wild habitat note: Unlike many Acropora species that prefer high-energy reef crests, Anacropora has been documented in muddy, non-reefal environments and lagoons, suggesting somewhat broader environmental tolerance in the wild. In captivity this may contribute to its reputation as slightly more forgiving than Acropora.
breedingNotes: Fragging is the primary propagation method in captivity. Colonies can be cut with bone cutters; frags encrust and attach to frag plugs or rubble. ORA (Oceans Reefs and Aquariums) has produced commercially available aquacultured Anacropora frags. Sexual reproduction occurs on natural reefs via broadcast spawning, but deliberate captive sexual breeding is not practiced in the hobby.

primary.name: Bulk Reef Supply – ORA Neon Green Anacropora
primary.notes: Primary source used for water parameters (temperature 76-80°F / 24.4-26.7°C, salinity 1.025-1.026, pH 8.1-8.4, alkalinity 8-12 dKH, calcium 380-450 ppm, magnesium 1200-1400 ppm), lighting (medium to high, 150-250+ PAR), flow (medium to high), and care level (moderate). LiveAquaria product page for Anacropora (product 7615) returned 404.

additional.name: Bulk Reef Supply – ECC Luminescence Anacropora
additional.notes: Cross-checked water parameters: temperature 75-78°F (23.9-25.6°C), salinity 1.024-1.026, pH 8.1-8.4, alkalinity 8-9 dKH, calcium 380-450 ppm, magnesium 1300-1450 ppm, PAR 150-350+. Minor disagreements on temperature upper bound (80°F vs 78°F) and alkalinity upper bound (12 vs 9 dKH) resolved by averaging: temperature 24-27°C, kH 8-10 dKH. Magnesium averaged across both BRS sources: 1250-1450 ppm. Both BRS sources agree on care level: moderate.

additional.name: Reef Chasers – Anacropora Coral Care
additional.notes: Confirmed PAR range 200-300, medium to high flow. Did not provide specific water chemistry numbers. Confirmed growth pattern, spacing requirement, and colony densification over time.

additional.name: Tidal Gardens – Anacropora Coral Care
additional.notes: Confirmed PAR 100-300 and medium to high flow. Provided native range (Fiji, Tonga, Solomon Islands, Great Barrier Reef). Confirmed morphological distinction: Anacropora lacks 'terminal polyps at the tips of each branch' (equivalent to axial corallites in Acropora terminology). Described as 'fairly hardy in comparison to some other SPS.' Did not provide specific water chemistry values.

additional.name: Wikipedia – Anacropora
additional.notes: Used for taxonomy (Order Scleractinia, Family Acroporidae), morphological description (absence of axial corallites, branches < 10 cm, porous areas between calices, rounded calices up to 1 mm diameter), native range (Indian Ocean and western Pacific), and habitat ('muddy waters,' non-reef environments). Approximately 7 species recognized including A. forbesi, A. matthai, A. puertogalerae, A. spinosa. Not used for husbandry parameters.
